behaviors.description: Clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract
behaviors.justification: The behavior tests whether the LLM can correctly extract key terms from a contract. A mock LLM would hide the real failure: an actual LLM's inability or inconsistency in finding these terms. Different models extract differently, and edge cases in real contracts won't be caught by a mocked 'always return this' response. Real-world failure would manifest when the LLM either misses terms or incorrectly parses clause boundaries—neither is detectable with mocked responses.
behaviors.test_description: Run the clause extractor on a test contract with manually verified ground truth (e.g., a 3-page contract annotated with all key parties, dates, and obligation clauses). Call the real LLM with the actual extraction prompt. Verify zero false negatives—all 100% of ground-truth terms appear in the extracted output. Run against multiple test contracts covering different term types and contract styles to ensure the LLM handles variations.

behaviors.description: Risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic
behaviors.justification: The core failure is that the LLM fails to detect indirect or euphemistic risk language (e.g., mistaking 'may have financial implications' for 'standard terms'). Mocking the LLM completely hides this—a mock always returns what you program it to. The behavior explicitly tests nuance detection, which requires a real LLM with knowledge of natural language and the ability to infer intent from euphemistic phrasing. No amount of local logic testing catches this; it's purely an LLM capability test.
behaviors.test_description: Create test contracts with euphemistic risk language (e.g., 'party may experience unfavorable outcomes' instead of 'substantial financial loss'). Configure a risk policy that marks certain financial impact statements as high-risk. Run the real risk assessor against these contracts and verify it flags the indirect language according to policy. A mock would trivially pass by returning 'high-risk' for every input.

behaviors.description: Compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: Compliance checking depends on the LLM's knowledge of jurisdiction-specific regulations (e.g., GDPR for EU, CCPA for California). A mocked LLM hides the actual failure: the LLM doesn't know the jurisdiction's requirements, or applies them incorrectly. Real failures would occur when the LLM misses a requirement or incorrectly assesses compliance. Only a real LLM call against a real or realistic contract can reveal whether it actually understands the jurisdiction's regulatory landscape.
behaviors.test_description: Create test contracts for specific jurisdictions (e.g., a data-processing contract for EU, a vendor agreement for California). Define ground truth: which regulatory requirements apply, which are met, which are violated. Run the real compliance checker with real LLM calls. Verify zero false negatives—all applicable requirements are assessed, and violations are caught. Test at least 2–3 jurisdictions with different regulatory complexity.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments, the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution
behaviors.justification: The merge logic combines multiple inputs (reviewer outputs) into a unified report. A real failure would be lost or mis-attributed assessments. This can be tested with mocked reviewer outputs—we don't need real LLM calls to test merge logic; we need controlled, conflicting inputs. The failure is in the merge algorithm itself (lost data, incorrect attribution), not in the LLM's ability. By providing two mock outputs that disagree on the same clause, we can verify merge correctness without LLM dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Create two mock reviewer outputs that flag the same clause with different assessments (e.g., clause extractor: 'standard indemnification', risk assessor: 'high-risk indemnification liability cap'). Run the merge step on these outputs. Verify the merged report contains both assessments, clearly attributed to their source reviewers, with no data loss or mis-attribution. Test multiple conflict scenarios (3+ clauses with different conflict patterns).

behaviors.description: Full pipeline—dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report—completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings
behaviors.justification: The full pipeline involves three independent agentic steps (clause extraction, risk assessment, compliance checking) dispatched in parallel, result collection (error handling, timeouts), and merge. Real failures include: one reviewer times out while others complete (collection logic fails), one returns an unexpected format (parsing breaks), or findings are lost during merge. None of these are caught by testing individual reviewers in isolation or by mocking the pipeline. Only running three real LLM calls in parallel against a real contract, with proper error handling and merge verification, will expose integration failures like incomplete result collection or silent data loss.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real, multi-page contract to the full pipeline. Run all three reviewers in parallel with real LLM calls. Collect results and merge into a unified report. Verify: (1) the report contains all findings from all three reviewers, (2) no findings are lost or duplicated, (3) conflicts are preserved with attribution, (4) the pipeline completes without error or timeout. Introduce variations: a contract with few clauses (all reviewers agree), a complex contract (many conflicts), and test with 5+ contracts to ensure consistency across contract types.
